coredata.dc:title: TME-responsive nanoplatform for multimodal imaging-guided synergistic precision therapy of esophageal cancer via inhibiting HIF-1α signal pathway

coredata.dc:creator: Ren, Guodong
Wang, Xuewei
Yang, Zhaobo
Li, Xiaowan

Liu, Wen
coredata.dc:description: Esophageal cancer (EC) is the sixth leading cause of cancer-related deaths, and its treatment poses significant challenges. In recent years, photodynamic, photothermal, and chemodynamic therapies have emerged as alternative strategies for tumor intervention. However, limitations such as poor tumor targeting, insufficient microenvironment responsiveness, and unclear mechanisms hinder their application. In this study, we found that hypoxia-inducible factor 1 alpha (HIF-1α) was highly expressed in clinical EC samples, which contributed to tumor malignancy and metastasis. We developed a carbon dots (CDs)-based tumor microenvironment (TME)-responsive nanoplatform, CDs-MnO2-Au-Cet (CMAC), designed for multimodal imaging-guided precision therapy in EC. Both in vitro and in vivo experiments demonstrated that CMAC effectively targeted and imaged EC cells and tissues. CMAC significantly inhibited tumor growth by inducing apoptosis and reducing lung metastasis. Mechanistically, CMAC administration led to a substantial downregulation of HIF-1α and its downstream targets, GLUT1 and MMP9. In summary, we presented a novel nanoplatform for imaging-guided synergistic therapy in EC, which demonstrated excellent anti-tumor growth and metastasis capabilities, along with favorable biocompatibility. This study laid the groundwork for developing innovative theranostic strategies for EC.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Esophageal cancer
Carbon dots
HIF-1α
Imaging-guided therapy
Metastasis

Cellular uptaken and tumor sphere penetration of CMAC
Performance of CMAC at the cellular level
In vitro anti-tumor capacity of CMAC
The anti-tumor molecular mechanism of CMAC
In vivo targeted imaging and anti-tumor effects of CMAC
Anti-metastasis effect and biocompatibiliy of CMAC

The authors gratefully acknowledge Prof. Li Lin of Xiamen University for the technical assistance.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Expression of HIF-1α and downstream molecules in clinical EC samples. a) The expression of HIF-1α in pan cancer in GEPIA data. (P values for comparisons among esophageal squamous cell carcinoma and paracancerous groups by t-test were indicated by asterisks: **P<0.01). b) The correlation among HIF-1α, HSP90, GLUT1 and MMP9 in STRING data. c) Expression of HIF-1α, HSP90, GLUT1 and MMP9 in clinical samples. (n = 5, Scale bar: 50 μm).

Characterization of CMAC. a) TEM images of CDs and CMAC. b) Absorbance spectra of different units (CDs, CM, CMA, Cet, CMAC). c) The dissolved O2 levels of different groups (0.1 mM H2O2, 150 μg·mL−1 CMAC and 150 μg·mL−1 CMAC with 0.1 mM H2O2). d) T1 signal of MRI of different concentrations of CMAC (0, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 350, 400 μg·mL−1) after incubating with/without 10 mM GSH for 30 min. e) Singlet oxygen production capacity of CMAC (150 μg·mL−1) detected by DPBF probe (60 μM) subject to 660 nm (0.5 W·cm−2) irradiation for 0–10 min. f-g) Photothermal heating curves and the corresponding thermal images of CMAC (150 μg·mL−1) with 660 nm laser irradiation at different power density (0.3, 0.4 and 0.5 W·cm−2). h) Absorption spectra of MB (0.15 mM) incubated with CMAC at different concentrations (0, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250 μg·mL−1). i) Generation of NO induced by 150 μg·mL−1 CMAC with 660 nm laser irradiation indicated by Griess reagent.

Cellular uptaken and tumor sphere penetration capacity of CMAC. a) Confocal fluorescence images of KYSE-30 cells incubated with 150 μg·mL−1 CMAC for different time. b) Flow cytometry analysis of KYSE-30 cells incubated with 150 μg·mL−1 CMAC for different time. c) Confocal fluorescence images of different cells (HEEC, KYSE-150 and KYSE-30) incubated with 150 μg·mL−1 CMAC for 4 h. (Scale bar: 20 μm). d) Immunofluorescence staining of EGFR expression in HEEC, KYSE-150 and KYSE-30. (Scale bar: 20 μm). e) Tumor penetration capacity of CMAC with or without 1 mg·mL−1 glucose in KYSE-30 cell spheroids. (Scale bar: 45 μm). The numbers in different depth of confocal images represented the penetration of fluorescence signal.

CMAC performance at the cellular level. a) Intracellular GSH concentration after co-incubation with different concentrations of CMAC (30, 60, 90, 120, 150 and 180 μg·mL−1) for 24 h. b) Intracellular H2O2 concentration after co-incubation with different concentrations of CMAC (30, 60, 90, 120, 150 and 180 μg·mL−1) for 24 h. c, f) CAT activity of CMAC at the cellular level. Rdpp (10 μg·mL−1) served as the oxygen probe. d, g) CLSM images and fluorescence quantitative analysis of NO generation induced by CMAC (5 μM DAF-FM DA served as probe). e, h) CLSM images and fluorescence quantitative analysis of ONOO− generation induced by CMAC (10 μM DHR 123 served as probe). (scale bar: 20 μm).

In vitro anti-tumor capacity of CMAC. a) Cell viability of KYSE-30 cells incubated with different concentration of CDs, CM, CMA and CMAC for 24 h with or without 660 nm laser irradiation (0.5 W·cm−2, 10 min). b) Annexin V-FITC/PI co-staining of KYSE-30 cells incubated with CDs, CM, CMA and CMAC for 24 h with/without 660 nm laser irradiation (0.5 W·cm−2, 10 min). c) Images of KYSE-30 cells stained with calcein AM (green, live cells) and propidium iodide (red, dead cells) after co-incubation with CDs, CM, CMA and CMAC for 24 h with or without 660 nm laser irradiation (0.5 W·cm−2, 10 min). (Scale bar: 200 μm). d) The mitochondria membrane potential evaluation of KYSE-30 cells treated with different drugs followed by 10 μg·mL−1 JC-10 dye. The green and red colors were generated by JC-10 dye in monomer and aggregated forms, respectively. (Scale bar: 20 μm). e) TEM images of KYSE-30 cells mitochondria in PBS and CMAC (150 μg·mL−1) with laser irradiation groups. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Anti-tumor mechanism of CMAC. a) Volcano map of differentially expressed genes (DEGs) of KYSE-30 cells incubated with 150 μg·mL−1 CMAC for 24 h and irradiated with 0.5 W·cm−2 660 nm laser for 10 min. The up-regulated genes are represented by red dots and down-regulated genes by blue dots. b) Gene Ontology (GO) enrichment analysis of DEGs between 150 μg·mL−1 CMAC with laser irradiation and control treatment group. c) Clustering heat map of DEGs. The redder the colour, the higher the expression level, and the bluer the expression level, the lower the expression level. d) The expression of HIF-1α, HSP 90, Cleaved PARP and Cleaved Casp3 in the PBS and 150 μg·mL−1 CMAC with laser irradiation groups. e) The immunofluorescence results of HIF-1α, HSP 90, Cleaved PARP and Cleaved Casp3 in the PBS and 150 μg·mL−1 CMAC with laser irradiation groups. f) Mechanism diagram of CMAC inhibition of EC cells proliferation and metastasis. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

In vivo imaging and anti-tumor effects of CMAC. a) In vivo fluorescence imaging according to pre-designed time points (0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 24 h) after intravenous injection of CMAC (200 μL, 10 mg·mL−1). And Ex vivo fluorescence distribution in tumor and major organs after intravenous injection of CMAC (200 μL, 10 mg·mL−1) at 24 h. b) MRI images according to pre-designed time points (0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 24 h) after intravenous injection of CMAC (200 μL, 10 mg·mL−1). c) Changes in tumor appearance of tumor-bearing mice in various treatment groups. (The treatment period was 21 days, the position of the red circle was the location of the tumor). d) The growth curve of tumors from ten groups after different treatments within 21 days. (n = 5, P values for comparisons among groups by t-test were indicated by asterisks: *P<0.05, **P<0.01 and ***P<0.001. *: other groups compared with PBS group). e) Tumor weight in each group at day 21. f) Changes in body weight in each group during treatment. g) Immunohistochemical analysis (Ki67, HSP90, Cleaved Casp3, MMP9, GLUT1 and HIF-1α) of tumor tissues after different treatments. (Scale bar: 100 μm). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Anti-metastasis effect and biocompatibility of CMAC. a) Treatment diagram of the anti-metastasis experiment. b) Changes in lung nodules appearance of tumor-bearing mice in various treatment groups. (Day 21, the position of the yellow circle was the location of the nodules). c) H&E staining sections of the lung in the PBS and CMAC with laser irradiation groups. (Scale bar: 100 μm) d) Serum biochemical indices (ALT, AST, CRE and BUN) of tumor-bearing mice after i.v. injection in different treatment groups for 21 days. e) H&E staining sections of the main organs (heart, liver, spleen and kidney) at 21 days. (Scale bar: 100 μm) (1) PBS; (2) PBS + L; (3) CDs; (4) CDs + L; (5) CM; (6) CM + L; (7) CMA; (8) CMA + L; (9) CMAC; (10) CMAC + L. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of the synthesis, and working mechanism of the cascade nanoplatform, CMAC, EGFR targeting synergistic therapy toward EC.

head.ce:title: TME-responsive nanoplatform for multimodal imaging-guided synergistic precision therapy of esophageal cancer via inhibiting HIF-1α signal pathway

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Basic Medical Sciences, Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan 030001, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Basic Medical Sciences, Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan 030001, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanxi Province Brain Degenerative Diseases Precision Diagnosis and Treatment Engineering Research Center, Shanxi Medical University, Jinzhong 030606, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanxi Province Brain Degenerative Diseases Precision Diagnosis and Treatment Engineering Research Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanxi Province brain degenerative diseases precision diagnosis and treatment engineering research center, Shanxi Medical University, Jinzhong 030606, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Cellular Physiology at Shanxi Medical University, Ministry of Education, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Cellular Physiology at Shanxi Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Cellular Physiology at Shanxi Medical University, Ministry of Education

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: G.R. and X.W. contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Esophageal cancer (EC) is the sixth leading cause of cancer-related deaths, and its treatment poses significant challenges. In recent years, photodynamic, photothermal, and chemodynamic therapies have emerged as alternative strategies for tumor intervention. However, limitations such as poor tumor targeting, insufficient microenvironment responsiveness, and unclear mechanisms hinder their application. In this study, we found that hypoxia-inducible factor 1 alpha (HIF-1α) was highly expressed in clinical EC samples, which contributed to tumor malignancy and metastasis. We developed a carbon dots (CDs)-based tumor microenvironment (TME)-responsive nanoplatform, CDs-MnO2-Au-Cet (CMAC), designed for multimodal imaging-guided precision therapy in EC. Both in vitro and in vivo experiments demonstrated that CMAC effectively targeted and imaged EC cells and tissues. CMAC significantly inhibited tumor growth by inducing apoptosis and reducing lung metastasis. Mechanistically, CMAC administration led to a substantial downregulation of HIF-1α and its downstream targets, GLUT1 and MMP9. In summary, we presented a novel nanoplatform for imaging-guided synergistic therapy in EC, which demonstrated excellent anti-tumor growth and metastasis capabilities, along with favorable biocompatibility. This study laid the groundwork for developing innovative theranostic strategies for EC.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Esophageal cancer (EC) is the second most common gastrointestinal cancer and the sixth leading cause of cancer-related deaths worldwide [1]. It often develops micrometastasis at an early stage, leaving only about 10 % of patients eligible for primary surgery [2]. For those with resectable EC, surgery is the primary treatment. However, approximately 80 % of patients experience recurrence or metastasis within two years post-surgery [3–5]. To enhance therapeutic efficacy, radiotherapy and chemotherapy have been utilized; however, chemotherapy often results in significant adverse effects due to off-target toxicity, while radiotherapy is limited by a narrow therapeutic window [6–9]. Thus, there is an urgent need for developing novel therapeutic approaches to improve treatment outcomes in EC.

In recent years, photodynamic, photothermal, and chemodynamic therapies (PDT, PTT, and CDT) have been developed and applied clinically for EC treatment. Bartusik et al. highlighted the potential of PDT for EC [10]; however, the hypoxic conditions and elevated glutathione (GSH) levels in EC cells can compromise its effectiveness. Additionally, the efficiency of CDT is often hindered by insufficient hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) within tumor cells [11–14]. Li et al. developed an acid-responsive nanosystem that combined anti-angiogenic therapy, PTT, and ferroptosis to inhibit EC [15]. However, the lack of tumor-targeting capacity raises the risk of off-target toxicity to normal tissues. Given that epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) is overexpressed in EC approximately 20 times higher than in normal tissues, EGFR represents a promising target for therapeutic intervention [16–18]. Cetuximab (Cet), an FDA-approved EGFR inhibitor, has been shown to bind effectively to EGFR, significantly improving the drug response and disease control rates in EC patients with metastasis [19]. This suggested that Cet could be used to target EGFR-overexpressing EC cells.

The tumor microenvironment (TME) comprises non-cancerous cells and components, characterized by hypoxia and elevated levels of GSH and H2O2. Research has demonstrated that the TME contributes to tumor growth and metastasis, making TME-targeted interventions become a promising cancer treatment strategy [20,21]. Manganese dioxide (MnO2), a common mineral, exhibits catalase (CAT) activity, converting H2O2 into O2 and enhancing aerobic therapies for tumors [22–24]. Additionally, MnO2 depletes excessive GSH in the TME and releases Mn2+, which can serve as an agent for magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and CDT [25–27]. However, the H2O2 consumed by MnO2 can diminish the effectiveness of CDT. Gold nanoparticles (Au NPs), nanoscale materials with glucose oxidase (GOX) activity, catalyze glucose to generate H2O2 [28,29]. The co-administration of MnO2 and Au NPs could create a synergistic effect in both CDT and PDT. The TME not only hinders the efficacy of oxygen-related anti-tumor therapies but also promotes tumor progression by stimulating the expression of proliferation-related proteins, such as hypoxia-inducible factor-1α (HIF-1α) [30]. HIF-1α is the primary transcription factor activated in response to TME hypoxia [31]. Under hypoxic conditions, proline hydroxylase (PHD) activity decreases, leading to stable HIF-1α production. Conversely, in normoxic or hyperoxic conditions, HIF-1α undergoes hydroxylation and is targeted for proteasomal degradation by E3 ubiquitin ligases [32]. HIF-1α plays a crucial role in tumorigenesis and metastasis by activating the PI3K-Akt-mTOR and Wnt/β-catenin pathways [33]. Furthermore, HIF-1α enhances metastasis by increasing the expression of glucose transporter 1 (GLUT1) and matrix metalloproteinase-9 (MMP9) [34–36]. Thus, HIF-1α is regarded as an ideal therapeutic target for EC.

To address these challenges, we proposed a novel theranostic nanoplatform, CDs-MnO2-Au-Cet (CMAC), aimed at improving efficacy of EC treatments. In CMAC, Cet acted as a targeting unit for drug delivery to tumors. Carbon dots (CDs) facilitated PDT, PTT, and gas therapy (GT). MnO2 functioned as a response unit and nanoenzyme, depleting excess GSH and enhancing PDT efficacy. The released Mn2+ was applicable for MRI and CDT, while oxygen production from H2O2 catalysis alleviates hypoxia, further improving PDT effectiveness. Additionally, the restorative fluorescence of CDs enabled near-infrared imaging (NIRI). Au NPs catalyzed glucose, reducing ATP production, downregulating HSP90, and amplifying PTT effects. GOX activity of Au NPs increased H2O2 levels, enhancing CDT and GT efficacy. In vitro and in vivo results demonstrated that CMAC achieved precise diagnosis and treatment for EC through multimodal imaging and synergistic therapies (Scheme 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validating expression of HIF-1α and its donwstream molecules in EC samples
ce:section.ce:para: Pathogenesis of EC is multiple factors involved. HIF-1α, one transcription factor induced by hypoxia, is proved to get involved in the occurrence and progression of variety of cancer including EC. To validate the critical role of HIF-1α in EC pathogenesis, we conducted a bioinformatics analysis using the GEPIA database to examine HIF-1α expression and its network in EC [37]. Our analysis revealed that HIF-1α expression in EC tissue was significantly elevated compared to normal tissue, showing the highest expression levels among different types of tumors (Fig. 1a). STRING analysis further indicated that HIF-1α interacts with key molecules associated with tumorigenesis and metastasis, such as GLUT1, MMP9, and heat shock protein 90 (HSP90) (Fig. 1b). Additional bioinformatics analysis confirmed the up-regulation of HSP90, GLUT1, and MMP9 in EC tissue (Fig. S1). To corroborate these bioinformatics findings, we performed immunohistochemistry (IHC) staining on clinical EC tissue samples. Consistent with our earlier analysis, we observed elevated levels of HIF-1α, GLUT1, MMP9, and HSP90 in EC tissue compared to adjacent non-cancerous tissue (Fig. 1c). These results suggested that HIF-1α might play a crucial role in the pathogenesis of EC and could serve as an ideal therapeutic target.

To develop a precise treatment for EC, we fabricated a novel nanoplatform composed of CDs, MnO2, Au NPs, and Cet. We first prepared CDs using methylene blue hydrate (MB) and L-arginine (L-Arg) under optimized conditions (Fig. S2) and subsequently analyzed their physicochemical properties. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) confirmed that the CDs were spherical, with an average particle size of 2 nm (Fig. 2a and Fig. S3a). Additionally, X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis revealed a strong diffraction peak at 2θ = 25°, corresponding to the (002) facet of graphite (Fig. S3b) [38]. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometry (FTIR) indicated that the surface of the CDs was rich in water-soluble functional groups, such as amino and carboxyl groups (Fig. S3c-h) [39]. Moreover, the CDs exhibited near-infrared (NIR) emission along with excellent photothermal, photodynamic, and nitric oxide (NO) production properties (Fig. S4). Importantly, the prepared CDs demonstrated good stability under various conditions (Fig. S5).

Next, to enhance the imaging and therapeutic performance of the CD-based nanoplatforms for EC, we incorporated MnO2, Au NPs, and Cet. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis revealed that the particle sizes of CM, CMA, and CMAC were 151 ± 1.4 nm (PDI: 0.211), 174.9 ± 1.2 nm (PDI: 0.344), and 194 ± 3.7 nm (PDI: 0.225), respectively (Fig. S6a-b). TEM confirmed that CMAC had a hollow structure with an approximate size of 128 nm (Fig. 2a). We also assessed the Zeta potential of CDs, CM, CMA, and CMAC, which indicated successful drug formulation (Fig. S6c). Additionally, absorption peaks specific to CDs, Au NPs, and MnO2 were observed in CMAC (Fig. 2b). XPS results revealed distinct peaks corresponding to Mn2p and Au4f (Fig. S6d-f), further confirming the successful preparation of CMAC. The stability of nanomaterials is crucial for their biomedical applications. We investigated the stability of CMAC in various environments and found that its size and Zeta potential remained unchanged in PBS, RPMI 1640, and 10 % FBS over 7 days, even after 48 and 96 h of freeze-thaw cycles (Fig. S6g-h).

Hypoxia and insufficient H2O2 in TME can hinder the effectiveness of PDT, and CDT of nanomaterials. To demonstrate CMAC's ability to overcome these challenges, we evaluated its CAT and GOX activities. As shown in Fig. 2c and Fig. S7a, the MnO2 and CMAC groups exhibited increased oxygen levels in the presence of H2O2, attributable to the CAT activity of MnO2. Furthermore, we assessed the GOX activity of Au NPs and CMAC by measuring H2O2 content using a commercial kit and the absorption intensity of potassium permanganate. The results in Fig. S7b-d demonstrated that glucose was effectively converted to H2O2 in the presence of Au NPs and CMAC. These results indicated that CMAC possessed excellent CAT and GOX activity.

Overload GSH is one feature of TME. So, we introduced MnO2, one established GSH-depleting mineral, to realize the TME responsibility of our nanoplatform. As shown in Fig. S7e, MnO2 was degraded in the presence of GSH, demonstrating its excellent GSH responsiveness. To validate the responsiveness of CMAC to GSH, we examined changes in Zeta potential and morphology in the presence of GSH (Fig. S7f-h). Increased concentrations of CMAC resulted in greater GSH depletion, and vice versa (Fig. S7i-j). These experiments confirmed the strong responsiveness of CMAC to GSH. The degradation of CMAC also facilitated its NIRI and MRI capabilities. We assessed the drug loading and release rates of CMAC, revealing a drug loading rate of 28.53 % for CDs (Fig. S7k). In the presence of 10 mM GSH, the release rate of Mn2+ was 84.40 %, ensuring the therapeutic efficacy of CMAC (Fig. S7l). Furthermore, Fig. 2d illustrated that CMAC exhibited a dose-dependent T1 MRI signal in the presence of GSH, highlighting its potential for EC diagnosis.

After confirming CMAC's responsiveness to the TME, we evaluated its photodynamic, photothermal, chemodynamic, and NO generation properties. As shown in Fig. 2e, ROS generated by CMAC increased with prolonged laser irradiation, as indicated by the DPBF probe. Additionally, CMAC demonstrated laser power- and concentration-dependent photothermal capabilities (Fig. 2f-g and Fig. S8a-b), with a photothermal conversion efficiency reaching 40.6 %, remaining stable over five cycles (Fig. S8c-e). Given the potential of photothermal agents for photoacoustic imaging (PAI), we assessed CMAC's PAI performance, which was concentration-dependent (Fig. S8f) [40]. The chemodynamic properties of CMAC were also investigated. As shown in Fig. 2h and Fig. S8g-h, CMAC generated singlet oxygen in the presence of H2O2 and GSH, with production positively correlated to the concentrations of both CMAC and GSH. The inclusion of L-Arg in CMAC facilitated NO generation, which was measured using the classical Griess reagent. As shown in Fig. 2i, NO production from CMAC increased gradually with longer irradiation times. Overall, the combined photodynamic, photothermal, chemodynamic, and NO-generating capabilities of CMAC highlighted its potential for effective EC treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptaken and tumor sphere penetration of CMAC
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the uptaken of CMAC by human esophageal squamous carcinoma cell lines (KYSE-30 cells), we performed confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) and flow cytometry analyses. The results indicated that CMAC was effectively taken up by KYSE-30 cells, achieving significant uptaken within 4 h (Fig. 3a-b and Fig. S9a). Cet in CMAC provided active targeting toward EGFR-overexpressing cells. As shown in Fig. 3c-d and Fig. S9b-c, KYSE-30 cells, which exhibited the highest EGFR expression, displayed the strongest fluorescent signal compared to normal esophageal epithelial cells (HEEC) with low EGFR expression and KYSE-150 cells with moderate expression. This observation was consistent with the western blot results (Fig. S9d-e). To investigate whether CMAC could penetrate tumor tissue, we established a KYSE-30 3D cell sphere culture. As shown in Fig. 3e, compared to CMAC alone, KYSE-30 cell spheres treated with CMAC and glucose exhibited stronger fluorescence signals and deeper penetration within the sphere, indicating enhanced uptaken and penetration capacity. This improvement could be attributed to the oxygen-generating properties of CMAC [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of CMAC at the cellular level
ce:section.ce:para: After confirming CMAC's GSH responsiveness, dual enzymatic activity, photodynamic, chemodynamic, reactive nitrogen production, and cell-targeting abilities, we further examined its performance in KYSE-30 cells. As depicted in Fig. 4a and Fig. S10a, CMAC effectively depleted GSH in KYSE-30 cells, as indicated by commercial GSH probes. Given the GOX activity of the Au NPs in CMAC, we assessed intracellular H2O2 levels in KYSE-30 cells treated with CMAC. As shown in Fig. 4b, H2O2 levels increased progressively with higher CMAC concentrations. Additionally, MnO2 contributed CAT activity to CMAC. As illustrated in Fig. 4c and f, CMAC with laser irradiation induced the highest oxygen generation levels, as indicated by the oxygen quenching probe Rdpp. Notably, more oxygen was generated with laser irradiation compared to the corresponding non-irradiated group, likely due to the photothermal effect of CMAC enhancing the oxygen generation process [42]. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. S10b, KYSE-30 cells treated with CMAC and irradiation produced the highest levels of ROS, as indicated by DCFH-DA, attributable to the excellent photodynamic and chemodynamic performance of CMAC. The presence of L-Arg in CMAC enabled NO generation, which can convert to peroxynitrite (ONOO−) in the presence of ROS. We detected NO and ONOO− using commercial probes DAF-FM DA and DHR 123, respectively. The fluorescence signals from these probes were strongest in the CMAC with laser irradiation group (Fig. 4d-e and g-h). These results collectively indicated that CMAC exhibited remarkable TME responsiveness, GOX/CAT activity, and effective production of photodynamic, chemodynamic, and NO/ONOO− at the cellular level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro anti-tumor capacity of CMAC
ce:section.ce:para: After validating the uptaken performance in EC cells, we assessed the anti-tumor efficacy of CMAC using MTT, colony formation, apoptosis, and calcein AM/PI assays. As shown in Fig. 5a, CMAC with laser irradiation exhibited the most significant cell-killing effect compared to CDs, CM, CMA, and CMAC without laser irradiation. The colony formation assay revealed the fewest colony units in the CMAC with laser irradiation group (Fig. S11a). Additionally, we investigated the impact of CMAC on the formation of KYSE-30 cell spheroids. As depicted in Fig. S11b, no visible tumor spheres were observed in the CMAC with laser irradiation group. To further confirm the anti-tumor efficiency of CMAC, we employed flow cytometry to quantify KYSE-30 cell apoptosis using Annexin V-FITC/PI staining. As shown in Fig. 5b, CMAC with laser irradiation resulted in the highest cell apoptosis rate at approximately 94.2 % with most of these occurring at the late apoptotic stage (83.4 %). In contrast, the apoptosis rates for PBS, CDs, CM, and CMA with laser irradiation were 5.74 %, 33.78 %, 68.75 %, 87.35 %, respectively. Fluorescence imaging was used to visualize the apoptosis induced by CMAC. As illustrated in Fig. 5c and Fig. S12a, the CMAC with laser irradiation group exhibited the strongest PI signal (indicating apoptotic cells) and the weakest AM signal (indicating living cells) compared to the other groups. These results demonstrated that CMAC with laser irradiation had a remarkable cell-killing effect on KYSE-30 cells.

As designed, CMAC generates ROS/RNS, which leads to mitochondrial dysfunction and subsequent cell death. To assess mitochondrial function, we utilized ATP, NADH, and JC-10 assays. As shown in Fig. S12b-c, CMAC with laser irradiation resulted in a significant depletion of intracellular ATP and NADH levels. Additionally, JC-10 analysis indicated that CMAC with laser irradiation disrupted mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP) (Fig. 5d and Fig. S12d). TEM further confirmed that the normal mitochondrial structure collapsed in the CMAC with laser irradiation group, with the mitochondrial cristae observed in the control group disappearing (Fig. 5e). Moreover, intracellular localization studies revealed that CMAC effectively targeted mitochondria, which likely contributed to the observed mitochondria dysfunction (Fig. S12f). This targeting ability was attributed to the positive potential of CMAC interacting with GSH in the tumor microenvironment (Fig. S7f) [43,44]. These results collectively demonstrated that CMAC induced apoptosis in EC cells through mitochondrial damage. Considering the biosafety for potential applications of CMAC, we examined its cytotoxicity on normal esophageal cells. As shown in Fig. S12e, CMAC had no significant effect on the viability of HEEC cells, indicating negligible cytotoxicity toward normal esophageal cells.

Metastasis is a characteristic feature of EC and a key factor hindering treatment effectiveness. To evaluate the anti-metastatic potential of CMAC, we conducted wound healing, invasion, and migration assays. Compared to the control group, CMAC with laser irradiation significantly inhibited wound healing, invasion, and migration of KYSE-30 cells (Fig. S13a-b), demonstrating its capacity to combat metastasis in EC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The anti-tumor molecular mechanism of CMAC
ce:section.ce:para: After observing the promising anti-tumor effects of CMAC, we aimed to elucidate the underlying molecular mechanisms. We performed transcriptomic analysis of KYSE-30 cells treated with CMAC and irradiation. Before conducting differential expression analysis, we performed principal component analysis (PCA) to verify the quality of the RNA sequencing data. PCA results indicated that samples within the control and CMAC treatment groups clustered together, while samples between the two groups dispersed significantly, confirming high-quality RNA samples (Fig. S14). As shown in Fig. 6a-b, treatment with CMAC and laser irradiation significantly altered gene expression, particularly in pathways related to cell proliferation, apoptosis, metastasis, and energy metabolism. The clustering heat map of differentially expressed genes (DEGs) revealed that CMAC with laser irradiation down-regulated anti-apoptotic and metastatic genes while up-regulating pro-apoptotic and anti-metastatic genes (Fig. 6c). To validate the expression of these DEGs, Western blot analysis was performed. Results indicated a significant down-regulation of HSP90, reducing the heat resistance of KYSE-30 cells and enhancing the PTT effect of CMAC. Notably, Cleaved Casp3 and Cleaved PARP, markers of apoptosis, were significantly up-regulated, while HIF-1α, GLUT1, and MMP9, which are associated with proliferation and metastasis, were suppressed (Fig. 6d and Fig. S15a, S15c-d). Immunofluorescence staining corroborated these findings, showing down-regulation of HSP90, HIF-1α, GLUT1, and MMP9, alongside up-regulation of Cleaved Casp3 and Cleaved PARP (Fig. 6e, Fig. S15b, and Fig. S15e), consistent with the Western blot results. Overall, these results suggested that CMAC with laser irradiation inhibited KYSE-30 cell proliferation and invasion by modulating apoptosis and metastasis-related pathways (Fig. 6f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo targeted imaging and anti-tumor effects of CMAC
ce:section.ce:para: The impressive in vitro performance of CMAC prompted us to investigate its anti-tumor effects in vivo. To determine its targeting capabilities, CMAC was injected into KYSE-30 tumor-bearing mice via the tail vein, and fluorescence imaging was performed at various time points using a multimodal Xtreme imaging system. As shown in Fig. 7a and Fig. S16a, CMAC dispersed throughout the body 2 h post-injection, with peak fluorescence signal in the tumor observed 10 h after injection, indicating an optimal time window for phototherapy. 24 h post-injection, the mice were euthanized, and heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney, and tumor tissues were collected. Notably, CMAC was highly enriched in the tumor, indicated by the strongest fluorescence signal. Fluorescence was also detected in the liver and kidney, suggesting that CMAC is primarily metabolized by these organs [45]. Additionally, we evaluated the MRI capabilities of CMAC. As shown in Fig. 7b and Fig. S16a, the T1 signal in the tumor peaked ten h after CMAC injection, consistent with the PAI results (Fig. S16b-c). These findings indicated that CMAC enabled multimodal imaging, including NIRI, MRI, and PAI of EC in vivo.

After confirming the imaging capacity of CMAC, we assessed its anti-tumor effects in vivo. Tumor-bearing mice were randomly assigned to ten groups (five mice per group), and CMAC administration was performed as illustrated in Fig. S17. The anti-tumor effects of CMAC were evaluated, with Fig. 7c visually demonstrating the most significant tumor inhibitory effect of CMAC combined with laser irradiation compared to other groups. This was corroborated by tumor volume and weight assessments. Treatments with CM and CMA, both with and without laser irradiation, showed moderate tumor inhibition, which was less effective than CMAC with laser irradiation but more effective than the PBS and CDs groups (Fig. 7d-e). Importantly, all treatments did not significantly affect the weight of the mice (Fig. 7f), indicating the excellent biosafety of CMAC. To further elucidate the anti-tumor mechanisms of CMAC, we performed IHC staining on tumor tissues. As shown in Fig. 7g, CMAC with laser irradiation down-regulated the expression of the proliferation marker Ki67 and HSP90, while up-regulating the apoptosis marker Cleaved Casp3. Additionally, anti-apoptotic proteins HIF-1α, GLUT1, and MMP9 were down-regulated. These results were consistent with hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) staining, which showed changes in cell density, and TUNEL staining, which labeled apoptotic cells (Fig. S18). Together, these findings demonstrated the excellent anti-tumor effects of CMAC and elucidated its underlying molecular mechanisms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-metastasis effect and biocompatibiliy of CMAC
ce:section.ce:para: Given that CMAC demonstrated strong capabilities in inhibiting xenograft tumors and suppressing metastasis-related proteins, we aimed to verify its anti-metastatic effects in vivo, particularly in the lungs, the most commonly affected organ. After tail vein injection of KYSE-30 cells, lung metastases were detected using CT imaging (Fig. S19), confirming the successful establishment of a lung metastasis tumor model. We then assessed the anti-lung metastasis effects of CMAC with laser irradiation according to the desigined protocol(Fig. 8a).

As shown in Fig. 8b, compared to the control group, both the number and size of nodules in the lungs of the CMAC with laser irradiation treatment group were significantly reduced. The anti-metastatic efficiency was further evaluated through H&E staining of lung sections. In the CMAC with laser irradiation group, only small metastatic loci were observed, whereas larger and more numerous metastases were present in the control group (Fig. 8c). To be noted, since 660 nm light had limited tissue penetration, the anti-metastatic effect of CMAC primarily resulted from enhanced CDT/GT. These findings indicated that CMAC effectively inhibited lung metastases.

To evaluate the biosafety of CMAC, we assessed blood biochemical markers, including AST, ALT, CRE, and BUN. As shown in Fig. 8d, all treatments did not produce significant changes in these indicators compared to the control group. Additionally, H&E staining of major organs, including heart, liver, spleen, and kidney, revealed no visible alterations in their histological structure (Fig. 8e). For a more comprehensive assessment of long-term stability and biosafety, we extended the duration of the in vivo study. We measured Mn2+ levels in the blood and major organs using inductively coupled plasma (ICP) analysis to evaluate CMAC metabolism in vivo. As shown in Fig. S20a, 24 h post-administration, Mn2+ levels returned to levels comparable to those in the PBS control group. Fig. S20b indicated that Mn2+ was primarily present in the livers and kidneys, where CMAC was metabolized. Furthermore, H&E staining confirmed that there were no observable changes in the major organs, reinforcing its excellent biosafety (Fig. S20c). Overall, these results demonstrated that CMAC was readily metabolized without significant accumulation in the body, indicating good biocompatibility. Therefore, the fabricated CMAC exhibited excellent biosafety and held significant potential for clinical treatment of EC.

Treatments for EC still face significant challenges. In this study, we developed a novel theranostic nanoplatform, CMAC, which exhibited several distinct advantages: 1) Excellent tumor targeting and TME responsiveness; 2) Multimodal imaging capabilities, including NIRI, MRI, and PAI; 3) Enhanced in vitro and in vivo anti-tumor efficacy through synergistic PDT, PTT, CDT, and GT; 4) Significant inhibition of lung metastases; 5) Mechanistic inhibition of proliferation and metastasis-related proteins such as Ki67, HIF-1α, HSP90, GLUT1, and MMP9, along with activation of apoptosis-related proteins like Cleaved Casp3 and Cleaved PARP.

This study paved the way for developing novel theranostic strategies for EC. It is important to note that a nanoplatform with absorption at longer wavelengths needs to be developed to enhance tissue penetration. The potential clinical application of CMAC will heavily depend on its efficacy and biosafety, which must be further validated in patient-derived xenograft models in future studies.

Guodong Ren: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Xuewei Wang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Zhaobo Yang: Resources, Methodology. Xiaowan Li: Resources, Methodology. Yingyu Ma: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Liang Zhou: Methodology, Investigation. Lili Yan: Supervision, Methodology. Sufang Ma: Supervision, Methodology. Lihong Li: Methodology, Conceptualization. Lixia Guo: Conceptualization. Boye Zhang: Methodology. Haipeng Diao: Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Haojiang Wang: Methodology. Bin Wang: Methodology, Conceptualization. Li Lu: Visualization, Supervision, Methodology. Chengwu Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Conceptualization. Wen Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
